I'm over here with the romance of why you want to be in Oklahoma. The salespeople are over with the 6% tax rebate for the first... Every state does that. Even horrible business states do that. That's like the minimum ante to get at the game table. We can assume. They know they can work you for a tax rebate. If he's a site selection consultant who is representing a company and looking at five states, he knows he can get me for a tax rebate. That's really not a question. But it's really the other stuff that will make somebody choose one or the other, want to be there. The tendency is to put way too much weight on facts, features, statistics, compliance, all of that. Not enough on the intangible, emotional, what does this mean to own this stuff? What's my outcome going to be? What's my transformation going to be? All of that. I know we're going over a lot of material here. What's really important is that you do read your transcript. That's why we had this transcribe for you. Dan went over all the elements of the offer. Unless you were taking crazy, fast notes, you're going to miss it. Just go back to your transcript and everything will be in there. We talked about benefits. I read something interesting. It was in the book Spin Selling. Great book on selling. What the author said, I think it's Neil Rockman. What he said is it's not a benefit unless your prospect says it's a benefit. Unless they agree that that's a benefit to them. Do you guys agree with that? Mostly. So that's what I call personal relevance. Okay, personal relevance. Perfect. And B2B selling, a very common mistake is dealing with corporate relevance and never getting to personal relevance. I hope everybody got that. That's key. He repeated again. Honestly, I want to make sure people got that. This is important. There's a tendency to talk about corporate relevance, relevance of benefits to the entity, and never getting to personal relevance for the human being or beings that are actually going to drive and sign off on this purchase. Just as Tip O'Neill famously said, all politics is local. All buying, if not personal, at least has a personal element in it. It arguably shouldn't in B2B. If you're a professional purchasing agent for XYZ company, you should be making all those decisions purely on corporate relevance, none on personal relevance, but you won't because you're not a robot. And hardly anybody has that kind of self-control. So your career interests, your personal and reputational risk of switching vendors, so a whole lot of personal stuff comes into this game. And so to that extent, I would agree with them. Another I would put some caution on the table is that often somebody doesn't know that a benefit is personally relevant unless the advertiser, marketer, copywriter, and or seller makes it personally relevant. Beautiful. And so the idea that agreement in advance, in a sense that we let the buyer define the agenda is, I think, a mistake. That's a really great distinction. So what you're basically saying is that the buyer is not educated enough in what you're selling possibly that they don't know that why the airbag, using a horrible example, or why the six airbags as opposed to the four airbags is a major benefit. You've got to tell them about that. So that goes, basically, a similar question, which is the hot button close. So first of all, describe what the hot button close and then let's talk about, well, is that true? Should we just be using the hot button close?
